title: SEGMENT 4 — Tasuku Honjo and the discovery of PD-1

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Tasuku Honjo and the discovery of PD-1
Tasuku Honjo is a quieter story.
Where Allison is the cowboy, harmonica-in-hand showman, Honjo is the meticulous, courtly Kyoto professor.
He spent his career working on immune-cell genetics, doing fundamental biology, and in 1992 his lab cloned a gene from a dying T cell.

They named the gene PD-1, for "programmed death 1," because the cell they cloned it from was undergoing programmed death and Honjo's group thought, reasonably, that the gene might be involved in apoptosis.
It wasn't.
That turned out to be a misnomer that stuck.
PD-1 is not a death gene.
It is a brake.
For most of the 1990s, nobody knew what PD-1 did.
It was just a gene Honjo's group had cloned — a sequence in a database, a molecule waiting for a function.
Honjo, patient as he was, kept pulling on the thread.
By 1999, his group had made PD-1 knockout mice — mice in which the PD-1 gene was deleted — and they discovered that those mice, like the CTLA-4 knockouts, developed autoimmunity.
Not as severe; the PD-1 knockouts didn't die in weeks.
But they developed lupus-like disease and arthritis.
The signal was clear: PD-1, like CTLA-4, was holding the immune system back.
It was a brake.
But it was, importantly, a different brake.
CTLA-4 acts early — during the priming phase, in the lymph node, when a T cell is first being activated by an antigen-presenting cell.
PD-1 acts late — out in the tissues, in the tumor, after the T cell has already been activated, traveled to the site of infection or cancer, and is in the middle of trying to do its killing job.
The mechanism is different too.
CTLA-4 competes with CD28 for the B7 ligand on antigen-presenting cells.
PD-1, when activated, recruits a phosphatase enzyme called SHP-2 directly into the T-cell receptor complex, and SHP-2 dephosphorylates the signaling molecules that the T cell needs to keep going.
PD-1 doesn't compete for a ligand.
It hits the T-cell signaling machinery directly, like reaching into the engine and disconnecting the wires.
And the ligands — this is the crucial part — the ligands for PD-1 are called PD-L1 and PD-L2, and they appear, naturally, on a wide variety of tissues, especially inflamed tissues.
Their job, normally, is to say to an arriving T cell: yes, there is inflammation here, but it's controlled, you can stand down.
They're the off-switch for a tissue response that has gone on long enough.
Tumors, of course, figured this out.
Many tumors — especially tumors that have already been infiltrated by T cells and are experiencing interferon-gamma signaling from those T cells — upregulate PD-L1.
They paint themselves with the molecule that tells T cells to stand down.
Every T cell that arrives sees the white flag, engages its PD-1 receptor, and gets the signal: don't kill this cell.
It's fine.
Stand down.
So Honjo's insight, parallel to Allison's: PD-1 is a second brake, working at the tumor site, often pressed by the tumor itself via PD-L1.
Block PD-1 — or block PD-L1 — and the brake is released.
The T cells, already present in the tumor, can kill.
Honjo's group, like Allison's, had to fight to get this idea into clinical development.
The story I've heard him tell — and you can find it in various Nobel interviews — is that pharmaceutical companies repeatedly turned him down.
He partnered with a small Japanese company called Ono Pharmaceutical, who in turn partnered with Medarex (the same company Allison had worked with), and eventually with Bristol-Myers Squibb.
The drug they developed, nivolumab, was an anti-PD-1 antibody.
Around the same time, Merck developed pembrolizumab — also anti-PD-1, from a slightly different parent program originally acquired from Organon and Schering-Plough.
Both drugs entered melanoma trials in the early 2010s, and both worked beautifully.
In September 2014, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for melanoma.
Three months later, in December 2014, it approved nivolumab.
The PD-1 era began.
And then the trickle became a flood.
Lung cancer.
Kidney cancer.
Bladder cancer.
Hodgkin lymphoma.
Head and neck cancer.
Hepatocellular carcinoma.
Esophageal cancer.
Gastric cancer.
Microsatellite-instability-high anything.
Triple-negative breast cancer.
Cervical cancer.
The list of indications has now exceeded forty cancer types and continues to grow.
Pembrolizumab alone is approved in something like fifty distinct clinical settings.
Honjo and Allison shared the 2018 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
The committee cited "their discovery of cancer therapy by inhibition of negative immune regulation.
" A clunky phrase, but a fair one.

They had discovered, independently, in different countries, on different molecules, the same principle: the immune system has brakes; cancer presses them; release the brakes, and the immune system can fight cancer.
That principle, alone, has changed the survival statistics for half a dozen major cancers.
We are still living inside the consequences.
